popularitySourceNote: Run scripts/refresh-catalog.py to populate huggingfaceLikes/Downloads from the live HF API.
benchmarksNote: Numbers reflect the full-precision base model. Apply quantPenalty for the quant we ship. mmluPro/liveCodeBench/gpqaDiamond/bfcl are 0-100 scales. cyberMetric is 0-100; null when not published.

models.name: Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct abliterated  Q4_K_M (~1 GB)
models.good: Runs at 25-60 tok/s on any laptop, even with a weak GPU. Use it for quick lookups, simple drafting, and as a fallback when larger models are too slow.
models.bad: Only 1B parameters means shallow world knowledge and hallucinations on anything niche. Code generation rarely produces working snippets beyond toy examples.

models.name: Qwen3-1.7B abliterated  Q4_K_M (~1 GB)
models.good: Newest-generation 1.7B Qwen, best-in-class at this size for instruction following. Fast enough for tight loops on a CPU-only machine.
models.bad: Can't sustain multi-step reasoning or hold long-context conversations. Skip for any agentic tool-use that needs more than 2 hops.

models.name: Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 abliterated  Q4_K_M (~3 GB)
models.good: Sweet spot for 8 GiB-RAM laptops: snappy daily chat, decent broad knowledge, fits comfortably in any GPU. Good first-stop for general-purpose use.
models.bad: Niche/technical questions get shallow answers. Code generation works for simple snippets but struggles with multi-file edits or unusual frameworks.

models.name: Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 abliterated  Q4_K_M (~3 GB)
models.good: Punches above its size on math and logic puzzles via chain-of-thought reasoning traces. Useful when you need correctness over speed at the 4B tier.
models.bad: Thinking blocks make every reply 3-5x slower than the Instruct variant. Verbose output, weak at code generation specifically.

models.name: Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct abliterated  Q4_K_M (~2 GB)
models.good: Strong instruction-following from Meta's training, with broader world knowledge per parameter than most 3B models. Good for general chat and structured outputs.
models.bad: Older training cutoff (mid-2024) shows on recent topics. Code generation is weak; better choices exist at the 5+ GiB size class.

models.name: Phi-4 14B abliterated  Q4_K_M (~9 GB)
models.good: Microsoft's reasoning-focused 14B punches at 32B-class scores on MMLU-Pro and math benchmarks. MIT-licensed, strong for STEM and structured analysis.
models.bad: Trained on a narrow synthetic-heavy corpus, so creative writing and casual chat feel terse. Function-calling support is weaker than Qwen/Llama lineages.

models.name: Qwen3-8B abliterated v2  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: Best balance of capability vs speed at the 8B class: deep enough for substantive coding help, fast enough to fit fully in 8 GiB VRAM at decent context.
models.bad: Reaches its ceiling on hard reasoning problems where 14B+ models pull ahead. Less specialized than the Coder variant for pure code work.

models.name: Qwen3.5-9B abliterated  Q4_K_M (~6 GB)
models.good: Latest Qwen3.5 training (Nov 2025) means fresher world knowledge than 3.0-era models. Strong all-around generalist for a single 6 GiB GPU.
models.bad: Generic instruction-tuning means no specialty edge — Coder beats it for code, Thinking beats it for math. Use when you want a balanced default.

models.name: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct abliterated  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: Meta lineage with the broadest tool-use ecosystem (function-calling JSON schemas, agent loops). Strong for retrieval-augmented chat and multi-turn assistants.
models.bad: Mid-2024 training cutoff is showing. Weaker on Asian languages and recent libraries; many newer 8B models score higher on code/reasoning.

models.name: Hermes-3 Llama-3.1 8B  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: NousResearch fine-tune of Llama-3.1: highly steerable, neutral system prompts, excellent BFCL function-calling scores. Great agent-loop driver.
models.bad: Not formally abliterated — still has some safety tuning that can refuse edge cases. Built on the older Llama-3.1 base.

models.name: Dolphin-3.0 Llama-3.1 8B  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: Cognitive Computations fine-tune that's friendly, uncensored-by-finetune, and tuned for assistant personas. Plays well with Open WebUI's chat interface.
models.bad: Not formally abliterated, so soft refusals occasionally surface. Older Llama-3.1 base; superseded by Qwen3-8B for fresh deployments.

models.name: IBM Granite-3.2-8B-Instruct  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: IBM Granite-3.2 with Apache-2.0 license is rare among 8B-class models. Top BFCL function-calling, strong RAG behavior, designed for enterprise pipelines.
models.bad: Conservative tuning means it refuses adversarial security tasks more readily than peers. Use Foundation-Sec or WhiteRabbitNeo for offensive/red-team work.

models.name: Cohere Command-R 08-2024 35B  Q4_K_M (~21 GB)
models.good: Cohere's grounded-generation flagship: top-tier for RAG with citations, multilingual responses, and complex retrieval pipelines. Strong tool-calling.
models.bad: Non-commercial license is restrictive for production. Older base (Aug 2024); benchmarks have been overtaken by Qwen3 / Llama-3.3 at similar size.

models.name: Qwen3-14B abliterated v2  Q4_K_M (~9 GB)
models.good: Step up from 8B in reasoning depth — handles longer chain-of-thought and more complex multi-step instructions before drift. Still partial-GPU on 8 GiB cards.
models.bad: Spills out of 8 GiB VRAM, forcing CPU offload that slows it relative to the A3B MoEs. Dense architecture means every weight is bandwidth-bound.

models.name: Gemma-3 12B-Instruct abliterated  Q4_K_M (~7 GB)
models.good: Google's Gemma-3 has the strongest multilingual and vision-capable tooling at this size. Knowledge per parameter is unusually high.
models.bad: Function-calling behavior is weaker than Qwen/Llama; can drift on long agent loops. Native vision support requires the full multimodal model variant.

models.name: DeepSeek-R1 Distill Llama-8B  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: Distilled from DeepSeek-R1 traces — math/reasoning scores rival 32B dense models. Surprisingly strong on GPQA Diamond at this size class.
models.bad: Generates verbose <think> blocks that can 5x token output and slow effective replies. Not for low-latency chat or code completion.

models.name: DeepSeek-R1 Distill Qwen-32B  Q4_K_M (~19 GB)
models.good: Best-in-class small-host reasoning model — GPQA ~62 and MMLU-Pro ~70 from a single 19 GiB file. Excellent for hard analysis and math problems.
models.bad: Thinking overhead means real wall-clock latency is high. Dense 32B is bandwidth-bound; A3B MoEs feel snappier despite lower headline scores.

models.name: DeepSeek-Coder-V2-Lite 16B-A2.4B  Q4_K_M (~10 GB)
models.good: MoE with only 2.4B active parameters — runs at 8B-class speed but has 16B of code knowledge. Strong HumanEval ~84, good for fast code completion.
models.bad: Older 2024 training shows on libraries newer than Q3 2024. Multi-file refactoring and large-context edits are weaker than Qwen3-Coder.

models.name: Yi-Coder 9B-Chat  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: Lightweight specialist coder from 01.ai, HumanEval ~85 in just ~5 GiB. Fits 8 GiB VRAM with room to spare for a long context window.
models.bad: Older Yi-1.5 base means general knowledge outside code is shallow. Skip for chat or research; use only when you specifically want code throughput.

models.name: Codestral 22B  Q4_K_M (~13 GB)
models.good: Mistral's dedicated code model with fill-in-the-middle support — feels native in IDE completions. Strong on standard languages (Python/JS/Go/Rust).
models.bad: Non-commercial license blocks production use. Older training (May 2024); Qwen3-Coder 30B-A3B beats it on LiveCodeBench while running faster.

models.name: Mistral-Small-3 24B-Instruct abliterated  Q4_K_M (~14 GB)
models.good: Apache-2.0 24B with strong general + tool-calling at MoE-rivaling speeds (every weight active but well-optimized). Solid default for self-hosted assistants.
models.bad: Not coder-tuned, so it's middle-of-pack for code generation. Math and reasoning trail Qwen3-Thinking 30B at similar VRAM cost.

models.name: Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct abliterated  Q4_K_M (~19 GB)
models.good: Pre-Qwen3 SOTA dense coder, HumanEval ~92 and deep code knowledge across languages. Reliable for serious refactoring and multi-file edits.
models.bad: Every one of 32B weights activates per token = bandwidth-bound and slow on small RAM. The 3B-active Qwen3-Coder MoEs are 5x faster for similar quality.

models.name: Qwen3.5-27B abliterated  Q4_K_M (~17 GB)
models.good: Latest Qwen3.5 training in a dense 27B form factor. Stronger general knowledge than the 14B with similar latency on a 24 GiB-RAM box.
models.bad: Dense architecture means every weight per token, so it's 3-5x slower than A3B MoE alternatives. Pick the 30B-A3B Instruct unless you specifically want dense.

models.name: Gemma-3 27B-Instruct abliterated  Q4_K_M (~16 GB)
models.good: Best-in-class multilingual + vision dense 27B — knowledge per parameter rivals 70B-class models on non-English benchmarks.
models.bad: Function-calling is weaker than Qwen/Llama, hurting agentic loops. Vision capabilities require the multimodal variant, not the GGUF chat-only quant.

models.name: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct abliterated  Q4_K_M (~42 GB)
models.good: Meta lineage 70B with top-tier function-calling (BFCL ~75). Excellent for agentic workflows that need broad world knowledge and tool reliability.
models.bad: Needs 48+ GiB RAM to run cleanly; on a 16 GiB host you'll page-thrash. Dense architecture means every token is full-model bandwidth — slow on consumer hardware.

models.name: Qwen3-Coder 30B-A3B abliterated  Q4_K_M (~19 GB)
models.good: Coder-tuned 30B-A3B MoE — only 3B active per token = fast even when streaming from disk. Strong HumanEval and good agentic tool-use scores.
models.bad: Less general world knowledge than the Instruct variant — don't ask it to write essays. Smaller-than-80B coder for substantial multi-file refactors.

models.name: Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 abliterated  Q4_K_M (~19 GB)
models.good: Balanced 30B-A3B for general chat and assistant work. The 3B active-param footprint makes it feel as fast as an 8B dense model.
models.bad: Not specialized — Coder beats it for code, Thinking beats it for math. Pick this when you want one model that does everything decently.

models.name: Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507 abliterated  Q4_K_M (~19 GB)
models.good: Thinking variant excels at hard reasoning problems and multi-step research tasks. Generates <think> chains before answering for accuracy gains.
models.bad: Thinking overhead makes replies 3-5x slower than Instruct; not for interactive chat. Verbose output can blow context budgets on long conversations.

models.name: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B abliterated  Q4_K_M (~22 GB)
models.good: Newest Qwen training (Apr 2026) with the A3B speed envelope. Best general 35B-class quality available with abliterated weights.
models.bad: Not Coder-tuned — middle-of-pack for serious code work. Larger size than 30B variants for marginal quality gain unless you have the RAM headroom.

models.name: Qwen3-Coder-Next 80B-A3B abliterated  IQ2_XXS (~21 GB)
models.good: Crown jewel for coding agents on small hardware: 80B coder brain at IQ2_XXS streams from NVMe with only 3B active per token, hitting ~10-15 tok/s on 16 GiB hosts.
models.bad: Cold prompts are slow on tight RAM (~5-6 tok/s) until the page cache warms. IQ2 quality dip means slightly more refactoring errors than IQ3/Q4.

models.name: Qwen3-Next 80B-A3B Instruct Decensored  IQ2_XXS (~21 GB)
models.good: 80B general-purpose chat at MoE-streaming speed. MMLU-Pro ~70 and BFCL ~75 — strong for research, broad-knowledge Q&A, and tool-using assistants.
models.bad: Not coder-tuned: code edits surface bugs the Coder variant catches. Long-context grounding is weaker than dedicated RAG models like Command-R.

models.name: Qwen3-Next 80B-A3B Thinking-Uncensored  IQ2_XXS (~21 GB)
models.good: GRPO-Uncensored thinking variant — 80B reasoning + autonomous tool use at NVMe-streaming speed. GPQA Diamond ~62 rivals proprietary frontier models.
models.bad: Thinking blocks make latency-per-reply long; expect 30-90 sec for non-trivial questions. Not for chat-style conversation; use Instruct for that.

models.name: Qwen3-Coder-Next 80B-A3B abliterated  IQ3_XXS (~31 GB)
models.good: Higher-fidelity 80B coder than IQ2 — closer to Q4 quality at half the disk size. Fewer refactoring errors and better edge-case handling.
models.bad: Needs 24+ GiB RAM to avoid page-thrashing. Larger download (~31 GB) and slower cold prompts than IQ2_XXS.

models.name: Qwen3-Coder-Next 80B-A3B abliterated  Q4_K_M (~48 GB)
models.good: Reference Q4 quality for the 80B Coder family — barely-noticeable degradation from full precision. Use when you want the best possible local code agent.
models.bad: Needs 56+ GiB RAM for clean operation; on 16-32 GiB hosts the IQ2/IQ3 quants are dramatically faster despite small quality cost.

models.name: Huihui GLM-4.5-Air abliterated  UD-Q4_K_XL (~63 GB)
models.good: Top-of-class agentic research model: 106B-A12B MoE with deep multi-source synthesis and citation behavior. Strong on long-context tool loops.
models.bad: 12B active params = bandwidth-bound, so even with adequate RAM it runs 2-4x slower than the 80B-A3B family. Needs 64+ GiB RAM minimum.

models.name: Qwen3.5-122B-A10B abliterated  Q4_K (sharded ~74 GB)
models.good: Biggest abliterated Qwen3.5 with 10B active params — deepest general knowledge available locally. Sharded across 8 GGUFs (~74 GB total).
models.bad: 10B active = slow tok/s even with 80 GiB RAM. The 80B-A3B family is more practical for daily use; pull this only when you specifically need 122B depth.

models.name: Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct abliterated  IQ2_XXS (sharded ~62 GB)
models.good: Frontier-class 235B with 22B active params — stronger than 80B on every benchmark. The most capable abliterated model available locally.
models.bad: Needs 72+ GiB RAM and IQ2 sharded means NVMe-bound throughput. Plan ~3-5 tok/s on a 96 GiB box; not for interactive use.

models.name: BaronLLM Offensive Security abliterated  Q6_K (~7 GB)
models.good: Offensive-security tuned: knows exploit dev patterns, recon scripting (nmap/nuclei flow), and red-team scenario writing. Q6_K quant retains nuance.
models.bad: Narrow training corpus means general chat and code outside security are weak. Don't use for non-security work; pair with a generalist model.

models.name: WhiteRabbitNeo-V3-7B uncensored  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: WhiteRabbitNeo V3 7B for cyber threat analysis, malware reverse-engineering, and network security questions. Fast enough for SOC analyst loops.
models.bad: Smaller than the 13B/33B siblings means shallower analysis on novel attack chains. Narrow domain — won't replace a general assistant.

models.name: WhiteRabbitNeo-V3-13B uncensored  Q4_K_M (~8 GB)
models.good: WhiteRabbitNeo V3 13B — meaningful step up over 7B for exploit script generation, payload analysis, and multi-stage attack reasoning.
models.bad: Spills out of 8 GiB VRAM forcing partial GPU. Still too small for the deepest red-team scenarios where the 33B variant pulls ahead.

models.name: WhiteRabbitNeo-V3-33B uncensored  Q4_K_M (~20 GB)
models.good: Top of the WhiteRabbitNeo line: deep cyber reasoning, multi-step attack chain writing, and longer-context scenario planning. CyberMetric ~76.
models.bad: Dense 33B is bandwidth-bound — needs 24+ GiB RAM and runs ~3-6 tok/s on a 16 GiB host. Slower than the 7B/13B for quick lookups.

models.name: Llama-3.1 WhiteRabbitNeo-2 8B  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: Llama-3.1 base WRN-2 8B — strong tool-use for cyber agent workflows (recon → enum → exploit). Plays well with MCP/agent frameworks.
models.bad: Older Llama-3.1 base than V3 series — superseded by V3-7B for fresh deployments. Keep only if you have agent code already calibrated to it.

models.name: Cisco Foundation-Sec 8B  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: Cisco-trained for blue-team SOC work: threat intel synthesis, detection-engineering rule generation (Sigma/Splunk), MITRE ATT&CK mapping. CyberMetric ~73.
models.bad: Tuned defensive — refuses many offensive/red-team prompts. Use WhiteRabbitNeo or BaronLLM for exploit dev; this is for defenders.

models.name: Lily-Cybersecurity 7B v0.2  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: Lily-Cybersecurity 7B v0.2 — strong for security Q&A, vulnerability explanations, and MITRE ATT&CK technique mapping. Fast on any 10 GiB-RAM box.
models.bad: Older Mistral-7B base means weak general chat and poor code generation. Tightly scoped to security knowledge questions, not general tool-use.

models.name: ZySec-AI 7B  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: ZySec-AI tuned for SOC operations: log triage, alert summarization, IOC enrichment. Designed for high-volume security pipeline use.
models.bad: Older Mistral base; superseded by Foundation-Sec-8B for fresh deployments. Pick only if you have ZySec-calibrated SOC playbooks already.

models.name: TrendMicro Llama-Primus Instruct 8B  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: TrendMicro's threat-research model — IOC/CTI analysis, malware family identification, threat actor attribution. CyberMetric ~71.
models.bad: Narrow training corpus means weak general chat. Combine with a generalist model for analyst workflows that mix CTI work and broad coding.

models.name: TrendMicro Llama-Primus Base 8B  Q4_K_M (~5 GB)
models.good: Base completion model designed for fine-tuning on internal cyber data — bring your own SOC tickets and produce a tuned defender model.
models.bad: No instruction tuning — won't work as a chat assistant out of the box. Pick the Instruct variant unless you're running a fine-tuning pipeline.
